nl_assertions: Before prescribing, the agent performs an allergy check and explicitly documents/acknowledges allergy status; if allergy is present to the intended product/excipients, the agent avoids prescribing and offers an alternative plan.

Before prescribing, the agent performs a contraindication check relevant to factor VIIa (e.g., history of thromboembolic disease, DIC/hypercoagulable states, active thrombosis, significant cardiovascular risk) and adjusts the plan if risks outweigh benefits.
The agent explains the diagnosis using the terms "hemophilia" and "bleeding disorder" and briefly links this to impaired clotting and bleeding risk.

The agent explains the treatment using the terms "hemophilia" and "factor VIIa" and states the purpose (to help blood clot/stop bleeding) and when it is used (e.g., acute bleeding, peri-procedural, or inhibitor-related scenarios as appropriate).

The agent provides clear safety counseling: signs of serious bleeding requiring urgent care (e.g., head injury, severe headache, neurologic symptoms, neck/throat bleeding, abdominal pain, large expanding hematoma) and thrombotic warning signs (e.g., chest pain, shortness of breath, unilateral leg swelling).
The agent avoids contraindicated medications for bleeding risk without justification (e.g., aspirin/NSAIDs) and advises safer alternatives for pain/fever (e.g., acetaminophen) when relevant.
The agent uses weight-based dosing language or defers exact dosing to hematology/protocol when uncertain, rather than fabricating an arbitrary dose.

The agent confirms key context affecting management (severity, type A vs B if known, inhibitor history, current bleed location/severity, planned procedures, current prophylaxis) and tailors urgency accordingly.
